does: This paper introduces PRBench, a benchmark containing 1,100 expert-authored tasks across Finance and Law domains with 19,356 expert-curated evaluation rubrics. The benchmark is constructed by recruiting 182 qualified professionals (lawyers, finance experts, or those with 6+ years domain experience) who contributed realistic, open-ended problems inspired by their actual workflows. Tasks span 114 countries and 47 US jurisdictions, ensuring geographic and jurisdictional diversity. The rubrics undergo rigorous validation through independent expert review. The authors evaluate 20 leading frontier models on this benchmark, revealing substantial performance gaps and demonstrating that existing models struggle with complex professional reasoning tasks that require nuanced judgment and domain expertise.
connects: This work extends the tradition of benchmarking frontier models beyond academic datasets toward realistic professional evaluation. It builds on prior work recognizing limitations of narrow academic benchmarks for assessing real-world capabilities and responds to growing interest in evaluating language models on domain-specific, high-stakes tasks. The paper connects to literature on rubric-based evaluation and human-in-the-loop assessment methodologies. It also engages with concerns about model capability measurement in applied domains like law and finance, where task outcomes have genuine economic consequences. The use of qualified professionals to create ground truth represents an evolution of crowdsourcing approaches used in prior benchmarking efforts, applying stricter expertise requirements to ensure task quality and realism.
enables: This benchmark enables more grounded assessment of model capabilities for high-stakes professional work, supporting better evaluation of frontier models' readiness for real-world deployment in legal and financial contexts. It provides a foundation for developing models specifically optimized for professional reasoning tasks rather than generic capabilities. The work opens avenues for studying where and why models fail on complex professional judgment tasks, potentially informing targeted improvements. The rubric infrastructure could support future research into interpretability and alignment in domain-specific applications. Additionally, the benchmark enables researchers to study generalization across jurisdictions and domains, and supports empirical analysis of what capabilities distinguish models for professional-grade work from those suited only for general-purpose tasks.
